keyFiles.purpose: VS Code extension entry point — registers all commands, dashboard, status bar, and handles the full command/lifecycle orchestration
Core orchestration engine — 6-phase run lifecycle: git checkpoint → memory check → plan gate → agent execution → quality gates → finalize
Agent definitions — system prompts, metadata (emoji/color/name), custom config loader, and the orchestrator system prompt
Standalone Cursor SDK agent that scans the workspace and produces a structured memory.json index for the project
Project memory persistence layer — reads/writes .orchestra/memory.json, exposes context string for LLM prompt injection
VS Code WebviewViewProvider — inline HTML/CSS/JS dashboard with Run, Status, History, Settings, and Memory tabs
Git safety layer — creates WIP commits before each run, finalizes commits after, and performs hard-reset rollback
Post-agent quality gating — detects and runs npm test/lint/build/type-check scripts in the user's workspace
Per-agent token and cost accounting with model-specific pricing; produces formatted summaries for the dashboard
Run history persistence — saves/loads/prunes per-run JSON files under .orchestra/runs/
Locates @cursor/sdk in the project's node_modules, global npm, or ~/.orchestra fallback location
Extension manifest — activation events, command palette entries, keybindings, sidebar view, and all VS Code configuration contribution points
Unit tests for agent prompt loading and custom config merging
Unit tests for memory read/write/clear/validation logic

architecture: Single-process VS Code extension using CommonJS modules; the extension host registers commands and a WebviewViewProvider, delegates to runner.js which executes a sequential 6-phase pipeline (checkpoint → memory → plan → agents → gates → persist), each phase handled by a dedicated module. All inter-module communication is synchronous require(); agent streaming is handled via the @cursor/sdk Agent.create / agent.send / run.stream() async iterator API.
patterns: Event-driven dashboard communication via postMessage (extension ↔ webview)
Phase-based pipeline with structured event emission (onEvent callback)
Module-level state reset pattern (custom agent config reloaded on every run)
Allowlist validation for agent names and model IDs (prototype-pollution prevention)
Graceful degradation (memory failure, missing SDK, non-git repo — all non-fatal)
JSON-in-markdown extraction with multiple fallback strategies
conventions: All source files are CommonJS (require/module.exports), no TypeScript or transpilation
File-level JSDoc block at top of each module describing its purpose
Logger callbacks (log, onLog, onEvent) passed as parameters rather than global singletons
Run IDs use format YYYYMMDDHHMMSS-XXXX (date prefix + 4-char random)
Workspace config stored under .orchestra/ directory (config.json, memory.json, runs/)
No npm runtime dependencies — only devDependencies; @cursor/sdk resolved dynamically at runtime
VS Code settings use 'orchestra.' prefix namespace
testSetup: Mocha + Chai; tests live in test/suite/*.test.js; runner at test/runner.js; executed via 'npm test' (node test/runner.js); 20+ unit tests covering git-checkpoint, quality-gates, run-store, cost-tracker, agents, memory-store, dashboard, analyzer-agent
buildSystem: vsce (Visual Studio Code Extension packager); 'npm run package' runs 'vsce package --no-dependencies --no-yarn --no-git-tag-version --allow-missing-repository' to produce a .vsix file; no bundler (webpack/esbuild) — source JS is shipped as-is
summary: Orchestra is a production-grade VS Code/Cursor extension (v3.0.0) that orchestrates multiple AI agents via the @cursor/sdk to implement full-stack features from a single natural-language prompt. It provides a 6-phase run lifecycle including automatic git checkpoints, an approval-gated execution plan, parallel specialist agents (backend, frontend, test, manager), post-agent quality gates, real-time cost tracking, and a persistent project memory index. The extension ships as a self-contained .vsix with no bundler; all modules are plain CommonJS and the @cursor/sdk is resolved dynamically from the user's workspace or global npm at runtime. A new developer should start with src/extension.js to understand command registration and then src/runner.js to follow the end-to-end orchestration pipeline.
